original_hypothesis.title: Share Manipulation Before distribute() Extracts Disproportionate Curator Rewards
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: An attacker can buy large quantities of shares for a credId just before calling distribute(), receiving a disproportionate share of accumulated rewards. After the 10-minute lock period, they sell their shares. If the reward exceeds the bonding curve spread and fees, the attack is profitable.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: distribute() reads share balances at call time. Share data is fetched atomically from Cred contract. The attacker can observe accumulated balanceOf[credId] on-chain and time their buy → distribute → sell sequence. The 10-minute lock period is the only mitigation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Reward distribution proportional to share count at call time; no time-weighted average or snapshot mechanism

preconditions: Attacker has ETH to buy shares on bonding curve
Rewards exceed bonding curve spread + protocol fees + creator royalties for round-trip trade

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: userRewards = (distributeAmount * userAmounts) / totalNum; snapshot at call time only
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No time-weighting means last-second buyers get same reward rate as long-term holders
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No snapshot, no time-weighted averaging, no minimum hold period for reward eligibility

analysis_questions: At what reward balance does the attack become profitable?
What bonding curve spread exists at various supply levels?
Can the 10-minute lock period be bypassed via TECH-H003?
validation_requirements: Calculate break-even: rewards captured vs bonding curve spread + fees
Simulate: buy 100 shares, distribute, wait 10 min, sell 100 shares

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Observe balanceOf[credId] = 10 ETH. 2. Buy 500 shares (dominating total supply). 3. Wait 10 minutes (lock period). 4. Call distribute(credId) to receive ~proportional share of 10 ETH. 5. Sell 500 shares. 6. Profit if reward share > bonding curve price impact + fees.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Extraction of rewards meant for long-term curators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Legitimate long-term curators who receive diluted rewards

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No economic simulation of distribute manipulation

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Depends on bonding curve price; potentially significant
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-transaction: buy → wait 10 min → distribute → sell
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Large share purchases followed by immediate distribution visible on-chain

validation_result.mechanism: just-in-time share acquisition for reward extraction
validation_result.impact_summary: An attacker can buy large quantities of shares immediately before calling distribute(), receiving a disproportionate share of accumulated curator rewards at the expense of long-term holders, because rewards are distributed proportionally to current share balances with no time-weighting or snapshot mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The distribute() function in CuratorRewardsDistributor.sol reads share balances atomically at call time and distributes accumulated rewards proportionally. There is no time-weighted averaging, no snapshot mechanism, and no minimum hold period for reward eligibility. The only mitigation is a 10-minute SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD that prevents immediate selling after buying, but crucially does NOT prevent immediate calling of distribute() after buying. This means an attacker can: (1) buy shares, (2) immediately call distribute() to receive proportional rewards, (3) wait 10 minutes, (4) sell shares. The net cost is the bonding curve spread plus fees; the net gain is the proportional share of accumulated rewards.

The attack is viable whenever accumulated balanceOf[credId] in the CuratorRewardsDistributor exceeds the round-trip trading cost (buy price impact + buy fees + sell fees). The attacker can monitor the on-chain balance and strike when profitable.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies CuratorRewardsDistributor.sol lines 77-118 as the vulnerable code. The distribute() function at line 77 reads totalBalance from balanceOf[credId], fetches current curator addresses and share counts from the Cred contract (lines 84-88), and distributes rewards proportionally to current share ownership (line 104: `uint256 userRewards = (distributeAmount * userAmounts) / totalNum`). No time-weighting, snapshot, or minimum hold period for reward eligibility exists.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Rewards are distributed proportionally to current share ownership (userRewards = distributeAmount * userAmounts / totalNum) with no time-weighting or snapshot. A last-second buyer gets the same reward rate per share as a long-term holder. There is no minimum hold period required before being eligible for rewards.
Permissionless share buying updates shareBalance immediately, making the buyer eligible for distribute() rewards in the same block.
The SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD only prevents selling for 10 minutes. It does NOT prevent calling distribute() immediately after buying. The attacker receives rewards instantly, then waits 10 minutes to sell.

calling_sequence: Step 1: Attacker calls Cred.buyShareCred(credId, largeAmount, maxPrice) with ETH - shares are immediately credited via _updateCuratorShareBalance (Cred.sol:394-407), lastTradeTimestamp is set (Cred.sol:397)
Step 2: In the SAME transaction or next block, attacker calls CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute(credId) (CuratorRewardsDistributor.sol:77)
Step 3: distribute() reads current share balances via credContract.getCuratorAddresses and credContract.getShareNumber (lines 84-88) - attacker's newly purchased shares ARE included
Step 4: Proportional rewards are calculated: userRewards = (distributeAmount * userAmounts) / totalNum (line 104) - attacker gets large share due to large holding
Step 5: Rewards deposited to PhiRewards via depositBatch (line 114) - attacker's reward is credited to balanceOf in PhiRewards
Step 6: After SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD (10 minutes), attacker calls Cred.sellShareCred(credId, largeAmount, minPrice) to recover capital
Step 7: Attacker calls PhiRewards.withdraw() to collect reward ETH
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical defect is in CuratorRewardsDistributor.distribute() at line 104: `uint256 userRewards = (distributeAmount * userAmounts) / totalNum`. This formula uses only the current share count with no temporal weighting. Combined with the fact that buyShareCred immediately credits shares (Cred.sol _updateCuratorShareBalance line 394-407) and distribute() has no access control or minimum hold requirement, an attacker who just bought shares receives the same per-share reward rate as long-term holders. The SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD (Cred.sol line 37: `uint256 public constant SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD = 10 minutes`) only gates selling, not reward eligibility.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script (Deploy.s.sol) shows CuratorRewardsDistributor is deployed with standard parameters. No special configuration affects this vulnerability.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
The attack scenario is valid and executable:

1. Attacker monitors balanceOf[credId] on CuratorRewardsDistributor - this is a public mapping, easily readable.
2. When balance is sufficiently large (exceeds round-trip trading cost), attacker buys large shares via buyShareCred().
3. Attacker immediately calls distribute(credId) in the same transaction or next block. There is NO lock period check in distribute() - it only reads current shares.
4. The attacker's reward is: (distributeAmount * attackerShares) / totalShares. If attacker buys enough to dominate totalShares, they capture most of the accumulated rewards.
5. After 10 minutes, attacker sells shares. The net cost is: (buyPrice + buyFees) - (sellPrice - sellFees) = bonding curve spread + all fees.
6. Profit = captured rewards - trading cost.

The 10-minute lock period is the only friction, and it doesn't prevent the reward capture - it only delays the sell. The attacker's capital is at risk to price movements during those 10 minutes, but with sufficient accumulated rewards, this is clearly profitable.

Note: The attacker also receives the caller royalty (withdrawRoyalty, default 1%) plus rounding dust from distribute(), further increasing profitability.
validation_result.impact_assessment:
HIGH impact. Long-term curators who hold shares and contribute to cred curation have their rewards diluted by just-in-time buyers. The lack of time-weighting means a buyer who holds for 1 second receives the same reward per share as someone who held for months. This undermines the entire curator incentive mechanism.

Specific impacts:
- Legitimate curators receive diluted rewards proportional to how much the attacker dominates total supply
- Repeated exploitation drains curator reward pools systematically
- Economic incentive to curate long-term is diminished
- No recovery possible - distributed rewards cannot be clawed back
recommended_mitigations: Implement time-weighted share balances for reward distribution (e.g., track share-seconds or use a checkpoint/snapshot mechanism)
Add a minimum hold period before shares become eligible for reward distribution (separate from the sell lock period)
Implement a snapshot mechanism that records share balances at a point in time before distribution, preventing just-in-time manipulation
Consider a commit-reveal scheme for distribute() where the snapshot is taken before the distribution transaction
Add a cooldown period after share purchases before the holder can call distribute()
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Cred, BondingCurve, PhiRewards, and CuratorRewardsDistributor contracts in a test environment. 2. Create a credId with some existing shareholders (e.g., 3 users each holding 10 shares). 3. Deposit significant ETH (e.g., 1 ETH) to CuratorRewardsDistributor for that credId. 4. From attacker address, buy a large amount of shares (e.g., 200 shares). 5. In the same block, call distribute(credId). 6. Verify attacker receives ~200/(200+30) = ~87% of the rewards. 7. Wait 10 minutes, sell 200 shares. 8. Calculate net profit = rewards_received - (buy_cost - sell_proceeds).
uncertainties: Exact profitability depends on bonding curve shape at various supply levels - higher supply means higher spread cost
Protocol fee percentage (deployed at 500 = 5%) and creator royalties increase round-trip cost
The 10-minute lock period exposes the attacker to bonding curve price risk if other users trade during that window
Gas costs on the target chain may affect profitability for smaller reward pools
additional_testing_needed: Economic simulation with realistic bonding curve parameters to determine minimum profitable reward balance
Test with various supply levels (low supply: cheap but high spread; high supply: expensive but low spread)
Test whether the attack can be combined with the caller royalty (1%) from distribute() for additional extraction
Verify the attack works across multiple transactions (buy in block N, distribute in block N+1)
